3: Arecanut (Areca catechu L.) is a tropical perennial crop belongs to Arecaceae family. In south-east Asian countries, arecanut is a commercially and socially important non-food crop. Areca palms are primarily grown for their dry kernel for mastication with betel leaves. Areca is the seed of areca palm and used mainly for chewing purpose daily by 600 million people worldwide (Nelson and Heischober, 1999). Arecanut is a common masticatory drug used in India, China and East Asia and the South Pacific. Through value

addition, all the parts of the palm can be utilized efficiently (Sadasivuni et al., 2015). The crop is grown either as sole or in mixture with coconut, pepper, cocoa, betel vine etc. The leading arecanut growers are India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Philippines. India leads the world in area (49.3%) and production (54%) followed by Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, however, the productivity is highest in China (2885 kg ha The world arecanut area was 7.03 million ha in 2005-06 but now reduced drastically to 0.96 million ha in 2016 (FAOSTAT, 2016). The major reason for the reduction in the area are increased production cost, stagnant production, reduced resource use efficiency, higher crop water requirement, erratic rainfall, price fluctuations and increased incidence of pests, and diseases. Agricultural practices form the part of the potential sources of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions with ~18% from India (INCCA, 2010), and in the world ~14% net global CO2 emissions coming

CML|institute of environmental sciences IPCC|intergovernmental panel on climate change CO2|carbon dioxide ISO|international standardization organization CRS|constant returns to scale kg|kilogram DEA|data envelopment analysis LCA|life cycle assessment DMU|decision-making units LCI|life cycle inventory Eq|equivalent MJ|mega joule FU|functional units PTE|pure technical efficiency FYM|farmyard manure TE|technical efficiency GHG|greenhouse gas

from this sector (Pachauri and Reisinger, 2007). India's GHG emis- sions rose by an alarming 4.7% in 2016 and India is the third largest GHG emitter after USA and China (Olivier et al., 2016). The majority of GHG emissions from Indian agricultural sector are occurring at manufacturing stage followed by inputs usage, farm mechanization (Pathak et al., 2010), and irrigation (Vetter et al., 2017). Over the years, awareness around the globe is increasing towards environ- mental safety and cleaner agricultural system (Khoshnevisan et al., 2014). Therefore along with energy efficiency, agricultural practices affecting the surrounding environment should be carefully considered (Mohammadi-Barsari et al., 2016).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) method is employed to assess the environmental impacts of a product during its life cycle. Previous works of LCA have examined the environmental impacts of agrarian activities in crops like peach (Vinyes et al., 2015), kiwifruit (Nikkhah et al., 2016), strawberry (Tabatabaie and Murthy, 2016), apple and peach (Vinyes et al., 2017), sunflower (Harris et al., 2016), grape (Litskas et al., 2017), organic tomato (Bosona and Gebresenbet, 2018), and carrot (Lopes et al., 2018). The LCA technique was adopted under different production systems to examine the step- wise environmental burden due to agricultural practices or inputs (Bojacá et al., 2014). LCA can also be used as a decision-making tool for policy-makers, administrators and for scientists to devise the climate change mitigation strategies under available production practices (Mohammadi et al., 2013).

On the other side, data envelopment analysis (DEA) is a linear programming methodology used to quantify in an empirical manner the relative productive efficacy of several similar entities named decision-making units (DMUs) (Cooper et al., 2007). DMU can include manufacturing units, production units, farms, firms, and hospitals etc. which are consuming identical inputs and pro- ducing identical outputs. The performance of DMUs is assessed in DEA using the concept of efficiency or productivity. DEA recognizes the efficient and inefficient DMU's, and also computes target effi- cient values for inefficient DMU's (Ramanathan, 2003). DEA has been applied in various crops such as rice (Chauhan et al., 2006), apple (Mousavi-Avval et al., 2011b), alfalfa (Ghasemi-Mobtaker et al., 2012), cucumber (Khoshnevisan et al., 2013a,b), kiwifruit (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2016a), tea (Kouchaki-Penchah et al., 2017), orange (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2014), rice (Nabavi- Pelesaraei et al., 2017), corn (Esfahani et al., 2017), and turnip (Khoshroo et al., 2018) production systems.

To assess the operational and environmental impacts of similar multiple units of a product or processes, DEA and LCA are used in combination (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2010). The combined approach provides eco-efficiency verification thereby enriches the interpre- tation of results (Syrrakou et al., 2006). Several previous studies have used a combination of LCA and DEA in the different agricul- tural production system such as in Fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2010), grape (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2012a), soybean (Mohammadi et al., 2013), paddy (Mohammadi et al., 2015), wheat (Masuda, 2016), cotton (Ullah et al., 2016), rice (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2017), organic blueberry (Rebolledo-Leiva et al., 2017), and dairy farms (Cecchini et al., 2018).

The plantation crops, due to the perennial nature, demand year- round management practices and hence have high energy and water requirements (Girgenti et al., 2013), sustainability issues and ecological impacts (Page et al., 2011). Furthermore, arecanut is one of the important commercial crop in the states of Goa, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Kerala, and Assam of India. The area under arecanut cultivation is increasing largely due to higher economic returns and is largely linked to loss of biodiversity and deforestation. The con- sumption of non-renewable resources such as chemical fertilizers, pesticide, and electricity are found increasing in arecanut produc- tion system and contributing for GHG emission. The energy flow and environmental impact are the key factors determining future sustainability of arecanut production due to its perennial nature. The sustainable arecanut production requires optimization of crit- ical resources and understanding of environmental impacts. Therefore, this study applied DEA and LCA to optimize the energy consumption and to assess the environmental impacts of arecanut production system.

Therefore, this study applied DEA and LCA to optimize the energy consumption and to assess the environmental impacts of arecanut production system.
7: <header>1. Introduction </header>

Arecanut (Areca catechu L.) is a tropical perennial crop belongs to Arecaceae family. In south-east Asian countries, arecanut is a commercially and socially important non-food crop. Areca palms are primarily grown for their dry kernel for mastication with betel leaves. Areca is the seed of areca palm and used mainly for chewing purpose daily by 600 million people worldwide (Nelson and Heischober, 1999). Arecanut is a common masticatory drug used in India, China and East Asia and the South Pacific. Through value

addition, all the parts of the palm can be utilized efficiently (Sadasivuni et al., 2015). The crop is grown either as sole or in mixture with coconut, pepper, cocoa, betel vine etc. The leading arecanut growers are India, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Malaysia, Indonesia, and Philippines. India leads the world in area (49.3%) and production (54%) followed by Sri Lanka and Bangladesh, however, the productivity is highest in China (2885 kg ha The world arecanut area was 7.03 million ha in 2005-06 but now reduced drastically to 0.96 million ha in 2016 (FAOSTAT, 2016). The major reason for the reduction in the area are increased production cost, stagnant production, reduced resource use efficiency, higher crop water requirement, erratic rainfall, price fluctuations and increased incidence of pests, and diseases. Agricultural practices form the part of the potential sources of greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions with ~18% from India (INCCA, 2010), and in the world ~14% net global CO2 emissions coming
Nomenclature

CML|institute of environmental sciences IPCC|intergovernmental panel on climate change CO2|carbon dioxide ISO|international standardization organization CRS|constant returns to scale kg|kilogram DEA|data envelopment analysis LCA|life cycle assessment DMU|decision-making units LCI|life cycle inventory Eq|equivalent MJ|mega joule FU|functional units PTE|pure technical efficiency FYM|farmyard manure TE|technical efficiency GHG|greenhouse gas

from this sector (Pachauri and Reisinger, 2007). India's GHG emis- sions rose by an alarming 4.7% in 2016 and India is the third largest GHG emitter after USA and China (Olivier et al., 2016). The majority of GHG emissions from Indian agricultural sector are occurring at manufacturing stage followed by inputs usage, farm mechanization (Pathak et al., 2010), and irrigation (Vetter et al., 2017). Over the years, awareness around the globe is increasing towards environ- mental safety and cleaner agricultural system (Khoshnevisan et al., 2014). Therefore along with energy efficiency, agricultural practices affecting the surrounding environment should be carefully considered (Mohammadi-Barsari et al., 2016).

Life cycle assessment (LCA) method is employed to assess the environmental impacts of a product during its life cycle. Previous works of LCA have examined the environmental impacts of agrarian activities in crops like peach (Vinyes et al., 2015), kiwifruit (Nikkhah et al., 2016), strawberry (Tabatabaie and Murthy, 2016), apple and peach (Vinyes et al., 2017), sunflower (Harris et al., 2016), grape (Litskas et al., 2017), organic tomato (Bosona and Gebresenbet, 2018), and carrot (Lopes et al., 2018). The LCA technique was adopted under different production systems to examine the step- wise environmental burden due to agricultural practices or inputs (Bojacá et al., 2014). LCA can also be used as a decision-making tool for policy-makers, administrators and for scientists to devise the climate change mitigation strategies under available production practices (Mohammadi et al., 2013).

On the other side, data envelopment analysis (DEA) is a linear programming methodology used to quantify in an empirical manner the relative productive efficacy of several similar entities named decision-making units (DMUs) (Cooper et al., 2007). DMU can include manufacturing units, production units, farms, firms, and hospitals etc. which are consuming identical inputs and pro- ducing identical outputs. The performance of DMUs is assessed in DEA using the concept of efficiency or productivity. DEA recognizes the efficient and inefficient DMU's, and also computes target effi- cient values for inefficient DMU's (Ramanathan, 2003). DEA has been applied in various crops such as rice (Chauhan et al., 2006), apple (Mousavi-Avval et al., 2011b), alfalfa (Ghasemi-Mobtaker et al., 2012), cucumber (Khoshnevisan et al., 2013a,b), kiwifruit (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2016a), tea (Kouchaki-Penchah et al., 2017), orange (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2014), rice (Nabavi- Pelesaraei et al., 2017), corn (Esfahani et al., 2017), and turnip (Khoshroo et al., 2018) production systems.

To assess the operational and environmental impacts of similar multiple units of a product or processes, DEA and LCA are used in combination (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2010). The combined approach provides eco-efficiency verification thereby enriches the interpre- tation of results (Syrrakou et al., 2006). Several previous studies have used a combination of LCA and DEA in the different agricul- tural production system such as in Fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2010), grape (Vázquez-Rowe et al., 2012a), soybean (Mohammadi et al., 2013), paddy (Mohammadi et al., 2015), wheat (Masuda, 2016), cotton (Ullah et al., 2016), rice (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2017), organic blueberry (Rebolledo-Leiva et al., 2017), and dairy farms (Cecchini et al., 2018).

The plantation crops, due to the perennial nature, demand year- round management practices and hence have high energy and water requirements (Girgenti et al., 2013), sustainability issues and ecological impacts (Page et al., 2011). Furthermore, arecanut is one of the important commercial crop in the states of Goa, Karnataka, Maharashtra, Kerala, and Assam of India. The area under arecanut cultivation is increasing largely due to higher economic returns and is largely linked to loss of biodiversity and deforestation. The con- sumption of non-renewable resources such as chemical fertilizers, pesticide, and electricity are found increasing in arecanut produc- tion system and contributing for GHG emission. The energy flow and environmental impact are the key factors determining future sustainability of arecanut production due to its perennial nature. The sustainable arecanut production requires optimization of crit- ical resources and understanding of environmental impacts. Therefore, this study applied DEA and LCA to optimize the energy consumption and to assess the environmental impacts of arecanut production system.

10: The survey was conducted in Goa state of India situated in Western Coastal Plains and Ghats zone of India. The surveyed area falls under Western Ghats and the region has two districts namely North Goa and South Goa. The mid lands, valleys and coastal areas are covered by arecanut while in the low lying areas, rice is culti- vated predominantly. Climatic conditions of Goa are hot and humid, with temperature and rainfall ranged between 17 and 35 °C and 2500-3200 mm annum-1 respectively. Soils of the region range from lateritic, coastal saline, clay to sandy type with a high content of ferric aluminium oxides. Most of the arecanut production sys- items are found in laterite soil which is acidic in pH. The data were obtained from the 70 arecanut farmers through a questionnaire in 2017-18 and verified by field visits.

14: For DEA analysis, the inputs and outputs showing environ- mental and economic impacts are used (Bosco et al., 2011). Fuel, water, electricity, pesticides, fertilizers, and manures were used as input and arecanut chali yield was the output. In this study, two DEA models such as Charnes, Cooper and Rhodes (CCR) and Banker, Charnes and Cooper (BCC) were used to analyze the efficiency of different DMU's. The CCR model is based on constant returns to scale (CRS) (Charnes et al., 1978) while BCC model is based on variable returns to scale (VRS) (Banker et al., 1984). The CCR and BCC models were used to calculate technical efficiency (TE) and pure technical efficiency (PTE), respectively (Azimi et al., 2017). Scale efficiency computes the variation of efficiency scores between CCR and BCC model and calculated as the ratio of TE to PTE.

Generally, the TE always lower than PTE, the value of TE and PTE varies between zero and one; where a value of one indicates the DMU is highly efficient. The values lower than one implies that the DMU is inefficient.

The standardized methodology of LCA and is described by ISO 14040-44 (Huang et al., 2018). A complete life cycle assessment consists of four coherent and iterative stages (1) definition of the goal and scope, 2) life cycle inventory, 3) life cycle impact assess- ment, and (4) interpretation of the obtained results (Nikkhah et al., 2018).
15: For DEA analysis, the inputs and outputs showing environ- mental and economic impacts are used (Bosco et al., 2011). Fuel, water, electricity, pesticides, fertilizers, and manures were used as input and arecanut chali yield was the output. In this study, two DEA models such as Charnes, Cooper and Rhodes (CCR) and Banker, Charnes and Cooper (BCC) were used to analyze the efficiency of different DMU's. The CCR model is based on constant returns to scale (CRS) (Charnes et al., 1978) while BCC model is based on variable returns to scale (VRS) (Banker et al., 1984). The CCR and BCC models were used to calculate technical efficiency (TE) and pure technical efficiency (PTE), respectively (Azimi et al., 2017). Scale efficiency computes the variation of efficiency scores between CCR and BCC model and calculated as the ratio of TE to PTE.

Generally, the TE always lower than PTE, the value of TE and PTE varies between zero and one; where a value of one indicates the DMU is highly efficient. The values lower than one implies that the DMU is inefficient.

The standardized methodology of LCA and is described by ISO 14040-44 (Huang et al., 2018). A complete life cycle assessment consists of four coherent and iterative stages (1) definition of the goal and scope, 2) life cycle inventory, 3) life cycle impact assess- ment, and (4) interpretation of the obtained results (Nikkhah et al., 2018).
16: <header>2.4.1. Definition of the goal and scope </header>

The goal of this study was an assessment of life cycle environ- mental impact of arecanut production systems. In this step, it is essential to identify the functional units (FUs) and the system boundaries (Khanali et al., 2017). In this study, two functional units (FUs) were considered viz. mass-based (one tonne of arecanut) and land-based (1 ha). Mass-based FU is more common in the LCA of agricultural production i.e. one-tonne production of the agri- cultural product has been used to the environmental assessment. In this study, system boundary was considered as all inputs from cradle (for example, production of chemical fertilizer from raw materials) to farm gate as well as the input consumption for are- canut production for example diesel fuel.
17: Life cycle inventory refers to quantify the inputs and outputs for each system (Firouzi et al., 2017). Cradle to farm gate environmental impacts of arecanut production can be classified into the back- ground (off-farm) and foreground (on-farm) emissions. Back- ground emissions include the pollutants from the production of material inputs, for example, generation of electricity and chemical fertilizers gas). Foreground emissions include the direct emissions from the consumption of inputs, for example, combustion of diesel fuel. Ecoinvent 3.0 database was used to determine the background emissions of arecanut production system.
18: Impact categories selection and characterization are the mandatory elements of the impact assessment in LCA; however, normalization, and weighing are the optional elements. In this
study, the IMPACT 2002 + methodology, which is a combination of IMPACT 2002, Eco-Indicator 99, CML and IPCC, was employed as it covers a variety of impacts and damage categories.

Various parameters may affect the final results of an LCA study. Different studies on the LCA of agricultural production systems have used different impact assessment methodologies. Because the selection of impact assessment methodology affects the final re- sults, an uncertainty analysis was conducted. In this regard, five other impact assessment methodologies including CML-IA base- line, EDIP 2003, EDP (2013), ILCD 2011 Midpoint, ReCiPe midpoint were considered to be evaluated by LCA. SimaPro software version 8.2.3.0 was applied to analyze the LCA.
19: This study applied DEA and LCA to optimize the energy con- sumption and environmental impacts of arecanut production. Fig. 1 illustrates the framework of LCA and DEA. More specifically, first, the energy flows of arecanut production were optimized by DEA. Then, the LCA was applied to determine the environmental impacts of arecanut production system. Then, the LCA analysis was done again using the optimized values which were developed by DEA.
20: The mean estimated quantity of energy inputs and outputs of arecanut production were summarized in Table 1. The results indicated that the mean labor and machinery power consumed in

the arecanut production were 6078 h ha 1 and 12 h ha -1 respec- tively. The majority of the labor power was used in basin prepa- ration, manure application, harvesting, and processing. However, machinery was used mainly for weeds management using grass cutting machine and for spraying fungicide. The share of total mean energy inputs in arecanut production is demonstrated in Fig. 2. The largest amount of energy consumption was attributed to the hu- man labor (19.3%) followed by FYM (18.8%), organic fertilizers (15%), irrigation water (13.6%) and nitrogen-based fertilizers (13.5%). The lowest energy consumption was due to herbicide which accounted for only 0.2% (1 kgha-1 of the total energy consumption.

The combination of FYM and organic fertilizers (33.8%) was accounted for the higher percentage of energy share, indicating the farmers of this region are depending more on recycling of residues and compost. A study in rice production system in India revealed that irrigation and fertilizers have the higher percent share of en- ergy (Chaudhary et al., 2017). In another study, the amount of en- ergy inputs consumed in different rice-based cropping systems were reported to be 44-54% for chemical fertilizers and 11-14% of labor in India (Yadav et al., 2017). The results also revealed that arecanut production system is energy intensive with a total energy input of 61706 MJ ha and output of 75416 MJ ha-1

The energy indices of arecanut production are presented in Table 2. The results indicated that energy use efficiency of arecanut production was 1.4. Pahlavan et al. (2011) reported lower energy use efficiency of 0.01 in greenhouse tomato production due to higher energy input. Due to higher energy consumption, the energy efficiency of arecanut production was found lower. Previous studies also reported lower energy use efficiency of 0.74 in cotton (Yilmaz

et al., 2005), 0.32 in tomato, 0.31 in cucumber, 0.23 in eggplant, 0.19 for pepper (Canakci and Akinci, 2006), 0.61 in eggplant (Ozkan et al., 2004). The energy productivity of arecanut was calculated to be 0.1 kg MJ which means 0.1 kg output was obtained per unit of energy consumption. Increasing the arecanut yield through better management practices will reduce the energy consumption especially through the judicious use of irrigation water, organic manures, and chemical fertilizers. As most of the farmers using sprinkler irrigation system, by adopting drip irrigation the water use efficiency can be improved and electricity usage will be reduced consequently. Soil tested based fertilizer and manure application will reduce the fertilizer and labor consumption considerably. The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. It is mainly due to the higher application of FYM and organic fertilizers in the system.

25: The average technical, pure and scale efficiency of arecanut farms is presented in Table 3. The results indicated that from the total of 70 farmers considered for the analysis, 28 farmers had the
Average technical, pure and scale efficiency of arecanut farmers (70 units).
|Average|Maximum|Minimum|SD Technical efficiency|0.89|1.0|0.62|0.12 Pure technical efficiency|0.96|1.0|0.68|0.09 Scale efficiency|0.93 (0.87)|1.0|0.66|0.09
*Figure in the parenthesis indicates the mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms.

TE score of one while the remaining 42 farmers secured their ef- ficiency scores less than one and were relatively inefficient in en- ergy use from the different sources. The average TE of arecanut farms was 0.89. This implies that same level of production could be achieved with 89% of the resources and 11% of resources can be saved if these DMU's were performed efficiently. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices and productivity of inefficient farms to increase the energy use efficiency.

The energy values that convert inefficient farms to efficient farm for reducing the present level of energy consumption were shown in Table 4. Based on results of TE, the realistic energy values to obtain the same level of output were determined using DEA. The mean operational reduction values indicated an excessive use of FYM (3916 MJI ha irrigation water (1827 MJ ha labor (885 MJ ha-1), organic fertilizers and electricity (586 MJ ha-1. So, adoption of the micro irrigation system and soil test based fertilizer application will help to reduce the energy consumption of arecanut production system studied in the region. Pahlavan et al. (2011) reported that reducing consumption of diesel fuel, electricity and fertilizers consumptions are important for en- ergy saving in greenhouse tomato production. Based on DEA re- sults, Mohammadi et al. (2013) suggested that reduction in the use of N-based fertilizers, irrigation water and electricity are necessary to achieve the efficiency of soybean farms.

28: Even though the DEA model identifies the efficient and ineffi- cient DMUs and ranks the inefficient ones based on their efficiency scores, but it fails to rank the efficient DMUs. In this regard, the super-efficiency analysis was carried out to determine the best performing super-efficient farms based on operational perspective. The super-efficiency analysis enables the identification of a smaller range of best performers, for detection of existing best manage- ment practices (Iribarren et al., 2010). The super efficiency computed for 28 efficient DMUs were presented in Table 5. If a cut- off criterion of super efficiency >1.20 (Mohammadi et al., 2013) is followed, only 9 arecanut farms were found super-efficient. These farms can be taken as benchmarks for proposing best management practices to enhance energy use efficiency (Wang et al., 2011).
29: The previous studies in different sectors used LCA+DEA and proven as suitable methodology for integrated assessment of environmental and economic issues in fisheries (Vázquez-Rowe
Table 4 Technical efficiency (TE) and operational reduction of inputs for the inefficient arecanut fields.

et al., 2010), watermelon (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2016b), aqua- culture (Lozano et al., 2009), organic blueberry (Rebolledo-Leiva et al., 2017) etc. The results revealed that major saving could be possible by the reduced consumption of FYM (203.9 $ ha year-1 and labor (176.3 $ ha- year-1) with a total mean economic saving of 413.4$ ha- year (Table 6). The economic estimation was made by considering the average price of the inputs in 2017, but the irrigation water is excluded as it is available naturally from wells. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2012b) reported an average annual savings of

400€ to 5150€ for inefficient vine farms. The economic reductions would be possible if the inefficient arecanut farmers adopted the improved management practices by considering soil test values, farm mechanization, drip irrigation, etc.

Table 5 Super-efficient scores for the farms that were detec- ted as efficient.

et al., 2010), watermelon (Nabavi-Pelesaraei et al., 2016b), aqua- culture (Lozano et al., 2009), organic blueberry (Rebolledo-Leiva et al., 2017) etc. The results revealed that major saving could be possible by the reduced consumption of FYM (203.9 $ ha year-1 and labor (176.3 $ ha- year-1) with a total mean economic saving of 413.4$ ha- year (Table 6). The economic estimation was made by considering the average price of the inputs in 2017, but the irrigation water is excluded as it is available naturally from wells. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2012b) reported an average annual savings of

400€ to 5150€ for inefficient vine farms. The economic reductions would be possible if the inefficient arecanut farmers adopted the improved management practices by considering soil test values, farm mechanization, drip irrigation, etc.

38: The characterization indices for arecanut production were dis- played in Table 7. The global warming potential, non-renewable energy consumption and ozone layer depletion for one-tonne production of arecanut were determined to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq, 12122.69 MJ, and 8.29 X 10-5 kg CFC-11 eq, respectively. However, the aforementioned amounts per hectare of arecanut were 2399.25kg CO2 eq, 30435.21 MJ, and 2.10 X 10 kg CFC-11 eq, respectively. The global warming potential for one tonne produc- tion of canola (Mousavi-Avval et al., 2017), pistachios and almonds (Bartzas and Komnitsas, 2017), rice (Nabavi-Pelesarae et al., 2018), soybean and sunflower (Dekamin et al., 2018) were reported to be 1182, 2119 and 2009, 1166, 1549 and 2283 kg CO2 eq, respectively. In this case, the amount of global warming potential of one tonne production of arecanut was less than those for aforementioned crops.
Table 6 Total Economic Saving (TES) linked to the accomplishment of operational targets.
Table 7 Characterization indices for arecanut production.

The contribution of inputs in the environmental impact of are- canut production system is demonstrated in Fig. 3. The results

highlighted that the nitrogen fertilizer consumption was the hot- spot in terms of global warming potential and non-renewable en- ergy consumption during arecanut production followed by electricity consumption. These results are in line with those of the previous study showing the high amount of N-based fertilizer combustion in the agricultural production supply chain (Nikkhah et al., 2017). Pishgar-Komleh et al. (2012) reported chemical fertil- izers had the highest rate of GHG emissions for potato production with 325 kg CO2 eq. ha-1; followed by diesel fuel. In another study, the results of GHG emissions analysis indicated the highest quan- tity of GHG emissions was belonged to electricity with 2004 kg CO2 eq. ha in wheat production (Khoshnevisan et al., 2013a,b).

However, the on-farm emissions were the main contributor in the respiratory inorganics, terrestrial acid/nutria and aquatic acid- ification impact categories (Fig. 3). With regard to ozone layer depletion, the pesticide consumption was found main contributor.

Table 8 shows the characterization indices of arecanut, calcu- lated by different impact assessment methodologies. This kind of uncertainty analysis can contribute to compare the findings of various LCA studies which have been done in crop production. The uncertainty analysis results indicated that the global warming po- tential for production of arecanut in 1 ha ranges from 2399.25 kg CO2 eq to 3239.251 CO2 eq. The results also suggested that the

highlighted that the nitrogen fertilizer consumption was the hot- spot in terms of global warming potential and non-renewable en- ergy consumption during arecanut production followed by electricity consumption. The characterization indices of arecanut production, calculated by different impact assessment methodologies.

Impact assessment|Global warming (kg CO2eq)|Ozone layer depletion (kg CFC-11 eq) CML-IA baseline|3238.39|2.10 X 10-4 EDIP 2003|3238.44|2.10 X 10- EDP (2013)|3238.39|2.10 X 10- ILCD 2011 Midpoint|3239.25|2.10 X 10-4 ReCiPe midpoint|3239.25|2.14 X 10-4 IMPACT 2002+|2399.25|2.10 X 10-4
* The bold numbers are the base scenario which was considered in this study.

ozone layer depletion ranges from 2.10 X 10 4 to 2.14 X 10 4 kg CFC-11 eq. These results are in line with those of the previous study (Rafiee et al., 2016) showing the selection of impact assessment methodology can affect the final results in an LCA study in agri- cultural sector.
The normalized damage assessment of arecanut production based on the different inputs consumption is illustrated in Fig. 4.

On-farm emissions of arecanut production had the highest negative effect on the human health damage category. However, nitrogenous fertilizer and electricity consumption for arecanut production had the highest negative effect on resource depletion damage category. Payandeh et al. (2017) observed nitrogen, machinery and diesel fuel as the main hotspot responsible for different impact categories in tea production. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2012a) opined that diesel fuel, fertilizers and pesticides usage were the main contributor for different impact categories and their usage should be revised to improve the environmental performance of grape production. Mohammadi et al. (2015) evaluated the environmental impact of rice production and found that the two main contributors to the GWP from rice cultivation was on-farm CH4 emissions followed by electricity for irrigation.

The normalized damage assessment of arecanut production system was illustrated in Fig. 5. The damage category of human health had the highest negative impacts during the production of arecanut in India. The direct emissions to air during arecanut pro- duction supply chain play a key role in the human health damage category. The damage category of ecosystem quality was ranked as
Fig. 4. Normalized damage assessment of arecanut production based on the different inputs consumption.
Fig. 5. Normalized damage assessment of arecanut production.

the second damage category with largest negative environmental impacts. This may be due to increased deforestation to expand the area under arecanut cultivation (Rao, 2011). The higher nutrient and water requirement of the crop contributing to the climate change and resource depletion. The N20 emission and eutrophi- cation are due to fertilizers and organic manure application and eco-toxicity due to use of pesticides (Brentrup et al., 2004) are the major damaging factors from arecanut production system.

The amounts of mitigation of environmental impacts based on the developed DEA model is illustrated in Table 9. The global warming potential, non-renewable energy consumption and ozone layer depletion mitigation per hectare of arecanut farms were calculated to be 11.64 kg CO2 eq, 44.29 MJ, and 1.77 X 10-7 kg CFC- 11 eq, respectively. Significant impact reduction was found in all the impact categories after optimization. By curtailing 11% energy consumption farmers can reduce the GHG emission considerably from arecanut production system in the region. Kouchaki-Penchah et al. (2017) reported that there is a good opportunity to mitigate the environmental impacts of crop production through integration of DEA and LCA. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2010) studied the perfor- mance of fishing vessels using LCA+DEA methodology and they opined that the total environmental impact for different impact categories was considerably lower after the energy optimization (Vázquez-Rowe 2012a). Revealed that reduction of 30% inputs usage in grape production system lead to 28%-39% of environ- mental gain. Payandeh et al. (2017) evaluated environmental
Table 9 Mitigation of environmental impacts based on the optimized scenario (per hectare).

Impact category|Unit|Amount Carcinogens|kg C2H3Cl|0.066 Non-carcinogens|kg C2H3Cl|0.94 Respiratory inorganics|kg PM2.5 eq|0.009 Ionizing radiation|Bq C-14 eq|23.73 Ozone layer depletion|kg CFC-11 eq|1.77 X 10-7 Respiratory organics|kg C2H4 eq|0.003 Aquatic ecotoxicity|kg TEG water|1538.28 Terrestrial ecotoxicity|kg TEG soil|2150.76 Terrestrial acid/nutria|kg SO2 eq|0.36 Land occupation|m² org.arable|271.54 Aquatic acidification|kg SO2 eq|0.05 Aquatic eutrophication|kg PO4 P-lim|0.03 Global warming|kg CO2 eq|11.64 Non-renewable energy|MJ primary|44.29 Mineral extraction|MJ surplus|0.34
Note- SO2: Sulphur dioxide; CFC: Chlorofluorocarbons; TEG: Triethylene glycol; C2H3Cl: Vinyl chloride; PM: Particulate matter; C2H4: Ethylene; PO4: Phosphate.

performance of 90 poultry farms and they appraised that by reducing 10.16% of energy consumption 12-57% of environmental impact can be reduced. The present study reveals that the arecanut farms in the region are having great potential for GHG emission reductions. The farmers' belief is that increased application of inputs will increase the crop yield and farm profit. Excessive use of chemical fertilizers, fossil fuel, pesticides, and electricity are becoming the major cause of GHG emission and climate change (Demirbas and Balat, 2009). As the studied area falls under coastal region, it is more vulnerable to climate change due to anthropogenic emission of CO2 by following faulty agricultural practices. To address the problem of climate change, long-term sustainable agriculture development is imperative. Although the consumption of chemical fertilizers is less, further reduction of fertilizers by organic source of nutrients might reduce the N20 emission considerably. The adoption of micro irrigation practices will help to curtail the electricity consumption and water consumption. Farm mechanization in certain operations particularly in weed management, harvesting, and processing will reduce the labor consumption. All the above interventions will lead to increase input use efficiency which will render the arecanut farms of the region as climate resilient system. The major draw- backs of combined application of LCA and DEA methodology are: (i.) optimum values derived from the methodology are region and management specific (Khoshnevisan et al., 2015), (ii.) lack of identification of the factors responsible for inefficiency, (iii.) the costs derived from optimization procedures are not known directly, and (iv.) depends on the sample size (Vázquez-Rowe and Iribarren, 2015).

The normalized damage assessment of arecanut production based on the different inputs consumption is illustrated in Fig. 4.

On-farm emissions of arecanut production had the highest negative effect on the human health damage category. However, nitrogenous fertilizer and electricity consumption for arecanut production had the highest negative effect on resource depletion damage category. Payandeh et al. (2017) observed nitrogen, machinery and diesel fuel as the main hotspot responsible for different impact categories in tea production. Vázquez-Rowe et al. (2012a) opined that diesel fuel, fertilizers and pesticides usage were the main contributor for different impact categories and their usage should be revised to improve the environmental performance of grape production. Mohammadi et al. (2015) evaluated the environmental impact of rice production and found that the two main contributors to the GWP from rice cultivation was on-farm CH4 emissions followed by electricity for irrigation.

The normalized damage assessment of arecanut production system was illustrated in Fig. 5. The damage category of human health had the highest negative impacts during the production of arecanut in India. Fig. 5. Normalized damage assessment of arecanut production.

the second damage category with largest negative environmental impacts. This may be due to increased deforestation to expand the area under arecanut cultivation (Rao, 2011). The higher nutrient and water requirement of the crop contributing to the climate change and resource depletion. The N20 emission and eutrophi- cation are due to fertilizers and organic manure application and eco-toxicity due to use of pesticides (Brentrup et al., 2004) are the major damaging factors from arecanut production system.

The amounts of mitigation of environmental impacts based on the developed DEA model is illustrated in Table 9. The global warming potential, non-renewable energy consumption and ozone layer depletion mitigation per hectare of arecanut farms were calculated to be 11.64 kg CO2 eq, 44.29 MJ, and 1.77 X 10-7 kg CFC- 11 eq, respectively. performance of 90 poultry farms and they appraised that by reducing 10.16% of energy consumption 12-57% of environmental impact can be reduced. The present study reveals that the arecanut farms in the region are having great potential for GHG emission reductions. The farmers' belief is that increased application of inputs will increase the crop yield and farm profit. Excessive use of chemical fertilizers, fossil fuel, pesticides, and electricity are becoming the major cause of GHG emission and climate change (Demirbas and Balat, 2009). As the studied area falls under coastal region, it is more vulnerable to climate change due to anthropogenic emission of CO2 by following faulty agricultural practices. To address the problem of climate change, long-term sustainable agriculture development is imperative. Although the consumption of chemical fertilizers is less, further reduction of fertilizers by organic source of nutrients might reduce the N20 emission considerably. The adoption of micro irrigation practices will help to curtail the electricity consumption and water consumption. Farm mechanization in certain operations particularly in weed management, harvesting, and processing will reduce the labor consumption. All the above interventions will lead to increase input use efficiency which will render the arecanut farms of the region as climate resilient system. The major draw- backs of combined application of LCA and DEA methodology are: (i.) optimum values derived from the methodology are region and management specific (Khoshnevisan et al., 2015), (ii.) lack of identification of the factors responsible for inefficiency, (iii.) the costs derived from optimization procedures are not known directly, and (iv.) depends on the sample size (Vázquez-Rowe and Iribarren, 2015).

The adoption of micro irrigation practices will help to curtail the electricity consumption and water consumption. Farm mechanization in certain operations particularly in weed management, harvesting, and processing will reduce the labor consumption. All the above interventions will lead to increase input use efficiency which will render the arecanut farms of the region as climate resilient system. The major draw- backs of combined application of LCA and DEA methodology are: (i.) optimum values derived from the methodology are region and management specific (Khoshnevisan et al., 2015), (ii.) lack of identification of the factors responsible for inefficiency, (iii.) the costs derived from optimization procedures are not known directly, and (iv.) depends on the sample size (Vázquez-Rowe and Iribarren, 2015).
7: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

8: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

9: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

10: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

tential of LCA + DEA in other agricultural production systems particularly in west coast of India.|sustainability of silage corn production by data envelopment analysis and multi-functional ecological footprint: evidence from sarayan county, Iran. 11: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

tential of LCA + DEA in other agricultural production systems particularly in west coast of India.|sustainability of silage corn production by data envelopment analysis and multi-functional ecological footprint: evidence from sarayan county, Iran. 12: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

13: <header>4. Conclusions </header>

This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

14: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

15: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

16: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

17: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

18: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

19: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

20: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

21: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-

22: This study optimized the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production system through integra- tion of data envelopment analysis and life cycle assessment. The information regarding inputs and out were collected from seventy arecanut farm in Goa state of India. The results indicated that the arecanut production system consumed total energy input of 61706 MJ ha with a total energy output of 75146 MJ ha The consumption of indirect (76%) and renewable energy (53%) was found higher in the arecanut production system. The mean scale efficiency of inefficient farms was found to be 0.87, indicating scope for improvement of management practices to increase the energy use efficiency. Under present condition, the global warming po- tential and non-renewable energy consumption for one-tonne production of arecanut were estimated to be 959.87 kg CO2 eq and 12122.69 M respectively. The energy optimization results indicated a good opportunity to reduce the energy consumption, environmental impacts and cost of arecanut production. In this regard, about 11% of the resources could be saved with a total economic saving of 413 $ ha year without reducing the are- canut yield. It shows that the energy optimization improves the efficiency of arecanut farms in economic perspective. The on-farm emission, nitrogenous fertilizer, and electricity were the main contributor in the majority of the impact categories. Arecanut production had the highest negative impact on human health fol- lowed by ecosystem quality. The efficient use of inputs in synchrony with crop requirement, adoption of drip irrigation, and effective machinery usage should be given priority to reduce the environ- mental impact of arecanut production system in the region.

LCA + DEA technique can be utilized for energy optimization and for environmental impact assessment in different production systems. Hence LCA + DEA methodology can be adopted by agri- cultural departments, policy makers, administrators, and re- searchers as a useful management tool to support decision making.

LCA and DEA may helpful to establish the critical values for envi- ronmental regulations in different agricultural production systems. To conclude, further research is required to harness the full po-
